answer:
I have everything I need. Here's the answer with citations to `command.go`.

## Declaring the two flags

```go
var rootCmd = &cobra.Command{Use: "app"}
var childCmd  = &cobra.Command{Use: "deploy"}

func init() {
    // --verbose: defined as a *persistent* flag on the root, so every
    // descendant command sees it (root, child, grandchild, ...).
    rootCmd.PersistentFlags().BoolP("verbose", "v", false, "verbose output")

    // --dry-run: defined on the child. Because it's persistent on the
    // child (not the root), only the child and its descendants see it;
    // siblings of the child and the root itself do not.
    childCmd.PersistentFlags().Bool("dry-run", false, "simulate without applying")

    rootCmd.AddCommand(childCmd)
}
```

Rule of thumb:
- **`Flags()`** – flag is local to *this* command only.
- **`PersistentFlags()`** – flag is local to this command **and** inherited by all descendants.

So put `--verbose` on the root via `PersistentFlags()` so every subcommand inherits it, and put `--dry-run` on the child via `PersistentFlags()` (or `Flags()` if you don't even want grandchildren to see it).

## The four accessors on `*Command`

These are all defined in [`command.go`](command.go) around lines 1688–1784. They return `*pflag.FlagSet`s but mean different things:

### 1. `Flags()` — line 1688
> "Flags returns the complete FlagSet that applies to this command (local and persistent declared here and by all parents)."

This is the **merged set used during parsing** — it contains everything this command can accept on the command line: its own local flags, its own persistent flags, and persistent flags inherited from ancestors (merged in via `mergePersistentFlags()`). When you write `cmd.Flags().GetBool("verbose")` inside the child's `Run`, this is what you're querying, and it works for both `--verbose` (inherited) and `--dry-run` (local).

### 2. `PersistentFlags()` — line 1775
> "PersistentFlags returns the persistent FlagSet specifically set in the current command."

The bucket you **add to** when you want descendants to inherit a flag. It only contains flags declared persistently *on this command* — not ones inherited from parents. This is where `--verbose` lives on `rootCmd` and `--dry-run` lives on `childCmd`.

### 3. `LocalFlags()` — line 1716
> "LocalFlags returns the local FlagSet specifically set in the current command."

Everything declared on this command itself, whether via `Flags()` or `PersistentFlags()`, **excluding** anything inherited from a parent. The implementation (lines 1731–1738) explicitly filters out flags that came from `c.parentsPflags`:

```go
// Add the flag if it is not a parent PFlag, or it shadows a parent PFlag
if c.lflags.Lookup(f.Name) == nil && f != c.parentsPflags.Lookup(f.Name) {
    c.lflags.AddFlag(f)
}
```

For the child, `LocalFlags()` contains `--dry-run` but **not** `--verbose`. This is what the help renderer uses for the "Flags:" section.

### 4. `InheritedFlags()` — line 1744
> "InheritedFlags returns all flags which were inherited from parent commands."

The complement of `LocalFlags()` — only the persistent flags pulled in from ancestors. Lines 1760–1764:

```go
c.parentsPflags.VisitAll(func(f *flag.Flag) {
    if c.iflags.Lookup(f.Name) == nil && local.Lookup(f.Name) == nil {
        c.iflags.AddFlag(f)
    }
})
```

For the child, `InheritedFlags()` contains `--verbose`. This drives the "Global Flags:" section of help output.

## Quick mental model

For your `child` command after the setup above:

| Accessor | Contents |
|---|---|
| `Flags()` | `--verbose`, `--dry-run` (everything parseable) |
| `PersistentFlags()` | `--dry-run` (declared persistent on this command) |
| `LocalFlags()` | `--dry-run` (declared on this command, any kind) |
| `InheritedFlags()` | `--verbose` (came from an ancestor's persistent flags) |

Invariants worth remembering:
- `LocalFlags() ∪ InheritedFlags() == Flags()` (disjoint partition).
- `PersistentFlags() ⊆ LocalFlags()` (a persistent flag declared here is still local *to here*; it just also propagates down).
- There's also a `LocalNonPersistentFlags()` (line 1702) if you want "local but not persistent" — i.e., things added via `Flags()` only.

For reading the values in `Run`, just use `cmd.Flags().GetBool("verbose")` / `cmd.Flags().GetBool("dry-run")` — `Flags()` is the merged view and is almost always what you want at runtime. The other three accessors are mainly for introspection and help-text generation.
